sections.title: Foundations of Autonomous Reasoning (1950s–1980s)
sections.description: The concept of machines that could act autonomously emerged with early AI research, notably the Logic Theorist (Newell & Simon, 1956) and the General Problem Solver (Newell, Shaw, & Simon, 1959). These works introduced formal planning and search algorithms that laid the groundwork for later agentic architectures.

sections.title: Symbolic Planning and Agent Architectures (1980s–2000s)
sections.description: Research on symbolic planners such as STRIPS (Fikes & Nilsson, 1971) and later the SOAR cognitive architecture (Laird, 1987) refined the idea of goal‑directed agents. The emergence of BDI (Belief‑Desire‑Intention) models (Rao & Georgeff, 1995) further formalized intentional agency in software agents.

sections.title: LLM‑Driven Reactivity and ReAct (2018–2022)
sections.description: The advent of large language models (e.g., GPT‑3, Brown et al., 2020) enabled zero‑shot reasoning, prompting the ReAct paradigm (Yao et al., 2022) that combined reasoning traces with action execution. ReAct demonstrated that language models could interleave thought and act, a pivotal step toward agentic behavior.

sections.title: Tool Use, Function Calling, and MCP (2023–present)
sections.description: Subsequent work integrated external tools, exemplified by the Toolformer framework (Schick et al., 2023) and OpenAI's function‑calling API (2023), allowing models to invoke APIs and retrieve structured data. The most recent milestone, the Modular Cognitive Planner (MCP) (Wei et al., 2024), unifies planning, memory, and tool orchestration, solidifying the emergence of fully agentic AI systems.
